card.title: When a Computer Beat the World Champion
card.teaser: On May 11, 1997, a 1.4-ton IBM machine did something no silicon had ever done before: it beat the best human on Earth.
card.coverImagePrompt: A tense 1997 chess scene: Garry Kasparov hunched over a board with IBM's Deep Blue cabinet looming behind him, cool blue light, grainy cinematic realism

lessons.title: The match the world watched
lessons.body: On May 11, 1997, in a Manhattan office building, Garry Kasparov — the highest-rated chess player in history — tipped over his king, stood up, and stormed off stage. He had just lost game 6 to a computer. It was the first time a reigning world champion had ever lost a classical match to a machine. The internet, still in its infancy, crashed under the traffic. Chess, and silicon, were never quite the same.

visual.imagePrompt: A 1997 newspaper front page announcing Deep Blue's victory, slightly faded paper, grainy film grain

lessons.title: What Deep Blue actually was
lessons.body: Deep Blue wasn't a thinking machine. It was a purpose-built chess monster — an IBM RS/6000 SP supercomputer with 30 PowerPC nodes and 480 custom chess chips that together evaluated about 200 million positions per second. Kasparov, by contrast, calculated perhaps 2 or 3 positions per second, but with the judgment of a grandmaster mind refined over 20 years.

keyTerms.definition: The scoring system a chess engine uses to rate a given position
The algorithm engines use to look ahead by assuming both sides play the best move available

visual.imagePrompt: IBM's Deep Blue — two tall black server cabinets in a bright computer lab, cables streaming

lessons.title: How the rematch played out
lessons.body: Kasparov had actually beaten an earlier version of Deep Blue 4–2 in 1996, but IBM rebuilt the machine with nearly double its speed and brought it back for a 6-game rematch. Kasparov won game 1 crushingly. Game 2 flipped: Deep Blue played a quiet, positional move — 36.axb5 — so un-computer-like that Kasparov later accused IBM of human intervention. Rattled, he resigned game 2 in a position modern engines have since shown was actually drawable. Games 3, 4, and 5 were drawn. Game 6 was over in 19 moves.

visual.imagePrompt: A dramatic chess board showing the key quiet move from game 2, arrows highlighting the surprising positional idea

lessons.title: A myth, decoded
lessons.body: Years later, Deep Blue's designers revealed the truth about move 44 from game 1 — a strange move that had frightened Kasparov into believing the machine was brilliant. It was actually a bug. Deep Blue couldn't pick a move and chose randomly. Kasparov, convinced he was facing a genius, changed his strategy in game 2 and started playing defensively — ultimately to his undoing. The greatest psychological lever in Deep Blue's game was an accident.
lessons.funFact: IBM's stock jumped about 15% after the match, adding billions of dollars in market value.

visual.imagePrompt: A close-up on a glowing computer screen showing an error message and a chess position

lessons.title: The end of one era, the start of another
lessons.body: After Deep Blue, chess changed forever. Engines became the coaches, the analysts, the training sparring partners. By 2017, a successor idea — AlphaZero — learned chess from scratch in 4 hours and surpassed every hand-crafted engine. Humans no longer beat computers at chess. Instead, we collaborate with them. The 1997 match wasn't just about a game. It was the moment machines stopped imitating us and started teaching us.

quiz.question: Approximately how many positions per second could Deep Blue evaluate?
quiz.explanation: Deep Blue's custom hardware could search roughly 200 million positions per second — vastly faster than any human.

quiz.question: What happened in the 1996 match between Kasparov and an earlier Deep Blue?
options: The match ended in a tie
quiz.explanation: Kasparov won 4–2 in 1996, but IBM upgraded the machine and returned for the famous 1997 rematch.

quiz.question: What was actually behind Deep Blue's unsettling move 44 in game 1 of the 1997 rematch?
options: A deep positional plan by IBM engineers
A hidden grandmaster in the room
A software bug that caused a random move
quiz.explanation: Deep Blue's engineers later revealed that the eerie move was caused by a bug — Kasparov simply read more into it than was there.

sources.title: Deep Thinking: Where Machine Intelligence Ends and Human Creativity Begins
